message.content:
The shop smelled of cedar shavings, brass polish, and the faint, metallic tang of old oil. Dust motes danced in the single shaft of afternoon sunlight that pierced the gloom, illuminating hundreds of ticking faces.

Seven-year-old Leo stood on a stool, his chin resting on the glass counter. Before him sat Elias, a man whose fingers were as gnarled as the roots of an ancient oak, carefully adjusting the escapement of a pocket watch with tweezers no thicker than a hair.

"Mr. Elias?" Leo whispered, afraid to disturb the symphony of ticks.

Elias didn’t look up. "Yes, Leo?"

"Why do they all tick at different speeds? The big grandfather clock in the corner goes *tock... tock...* so slow. But this little one goes *tick-tick-tick-tick* so fast. Is time faster here?"

Elias finally looked up, his eyes magnified behind thick spectacles. A small smile crinkled the corners of his mouth. "That is a very good question. Come here. Closer."

Leo leaned in. The pocket watch was open, revealing a universe of tiny gears, springs, and jewels.

"Look at this spring," Elias said, pointing with the tip of his tweezers. "It wants to unwind. It wants to let go of all its energy all at once. *Zap!* Gone. But if it did that, the watch would be useless. It would tell you nothing."

"So the gears stop it?" Leo asked.

"The gears *measure* it," Elias corrected gently. "Time isn’t the ticking, Leo. Time is the space between the ticks. The ticking is just us trying to catch it."

Leo frowned, confused. "But if I run, time feels fast. And if I’m waiting for my mom
